nativeRange.habitat: The Trout Goodeid inhabits fast-flowing rivers, streams, and spring-fed creeks in west-central Mexico, where the water is clear, well-oxygenated, and typically running over rocky or gravel substrate with moderate to strong current. Riparian vegetation along stream margins is a common feature of occupied sites.

decorPreferences: smooth rocks and pebbles replicating Mexican highland stream substrate
strong water flow via powerhead or canister filter outlet
hardy plants (Java fern, Anubias, Vallisneria) tolerant of current
open mid-water swimming lanes for active schooling
driftwood or slate for territory delineation among males

diet.notes: Omnivore with a strong herbivorous tendency mirroring wild feeding on algae and plant matter. Offer a staple of spirulina flakes or algae-based pellets, supplemented with blanched vegetables (zucchini, spinach), and occasional frozen or live foods (brine shrimp, daphnia). Will graze soft aquatic plants if underfed on vegetable matter; ensuring adequate plant-based food prevents unwanted plant damage.

goodWith: other cool-water livebearers (Xenotoca, Zoogoneticus, Ameca splendens)
fast-moving danios and white cloud mountain minnows
avoidWith: slow-moving or long-finned species susceptible to fin nipping
delicate or timid fish stressed by high activity levels
very small fish or fry that may be predated

imageCandidates.notes: Photo by Andrewbogott; 3358x1987. Two live Ilyodon furcidens in sharp focus, well-lit against dark background, clearly showing the diagnostic trout-like speckled patterning and golden 'goldbreast' tones along the flanks. Whole fish in frame.

summary: The Trout Goodeid (Ilyodon furcidens) is an active, cool-water livebearer from highland rivers in Mexico, named for its trout-like speckled patterning. Females nourish young internally via specialised placenta-like structures unique to the Goodeidae family. The species is valued by conservation hobbyists, as wild populations have declined due to invasive species and habitat loss.
careNotes: The Trout Goodeid (Ilyodon furcidens) is a robust, active livebearer native to the fast-flowing highland rivers and streams of the Armería and Ameca-Magdalena drainages in the Mexican states of Jalisco and Colima. It is one of only two indisputably valid species in the genus Ilyodon and is also commonly sold as the Goldbreast Splitfin or Goldbreast Goodeid. The species name "furcidens" refers to its distinctive fork-shaped teeth. A key husbandry requirement is cool water: the Trout Goodeid thrives between 18–24°C and does not need a heater in most temperate indoor environments. Temperatures above 26°C are associated with stress, shortened lifespan, and reproductive failure. Strong water flow and high oxygenation are essential — a powerhead or canister filter creating a noticeable current replicates wild conditions and keeps the fish healthy. A tank of at least 150 L with a footprint of 90 cm or more is recommended for a group of 6+, which is the minimum to allow natural shoaling behaviour and to spread male-on-male and male-on-female conspecific aggression. Males may develop a mild cervical (nuchal) hump with age, which is normal. The diet should emphasise plant and algae-based foods — spirulina flakes, algae wafers, and blanched vegetables — supplemented with high-quality flake and occasional frozen foods. Underfeeding on vegetable matter may result in plant nibbling. Water parameters should be maintained at moderate to hard hardness (gH 10–20) and slightly alkaline to neutral pH (7.0–8.0), reflecting the mineral-rich waters of their highland Mexican habitat. The species is widely kept by conservation hobbyists participating in coordinated breeding programmes for Mexican Goodeidae. Wild populations in the Ayuquila River have been severely impacted by invasive largemouth bass (Micropterus salmoides) and Poeciliopsis gracilis, making captive stocks important for the species\u2019 long-term security.
breedingNotes: The Trout Goodeid is a true livebearer producing live, well-developed fry via an internal placenta-like structure called the trophotaenia — a specialised nourishing connection unique to the Goodeidae family. Unlike poeciliid livebearers (guppies, mollies), females cannot store sperm and must be remated for each brood, making continuous mixed-sex groups the standard keeping arrangement. Gestation lasts approximately 7–8 weeks, with broods typically numbering 5–50 fry depending on female size and condition. Fry are born large and immediately capable of accepting adult-sized foods, micro-pellets, and baby brine shrimp. Parental predation of fry is low compared to many livebearers, but some cover (floating plants, dense vegetation) is recommended to improve fry survival in a community setting. Breeding is most reliable at the cooler end of the temperature range (18–22°C) with a varied, plant-rich diet. Breeding difficulty is rated moderate due to the cool-water requirement and the need to maintain stable, well-oxygenated conditions.

primary.name: SeriouslyFish — Ilyodon furcidens
primary.notes: Primary source. Provided adult size (males 7 cm, females 9 cm), temperature 18–24°C, pH 7.0–8.0, hardness 10–20 dH, native range (Jalisco and Colima, Mexico), tank dimensions (min 75×30×30 cm), diet (omnivore/herbivore tendency), breeding notes (trophotaeniae, 7–8 week gestation, 5–50 fry, no sperm storage), and species name etymology (fork-shaped teeth).

additional.name: FishBase — Ilyodon furcidens
additional.notes: Cross-checked taxonomy (Goodeidae, Cyprinodontiformes), size (males 7 cm, females 9 cm TL), native range (Ameca-Magdalena and Armeria-Coahuayana river basins, Mexico), IUCN status (Least Concern, assessed 16 March 2018), trophic level 2.0 (herbivore/omnivore). FishBase lists temperature as 24–27°C which conflicts with hobbyist-consensus cool-water (18–24°C) from SeriouslyFish; SeriouslyFish values adopted as primary for aquarium guidance.

additional.name: Steenfott Aquatics — Trout Goodeid Care Guide
additional.notes: Confirmed cool-water requirement (65–75°F / 18–24°C), minimum 40-gallon tank, active mid-to-upper swimmer, semi-aggressive toward slow or delicate tankmates, omnivore with herbivorous preference, noted as "constant grazer" that will nip plants if underfed, groups of 6+ recommended.

additional.name: PMC — Distribution and Conservation Status of Mexican Goodeidae
additional.notes: Peer-reviewed conservation paper. Confirmed IUCN Least Concern (2018) with declining population trend. Documents near-elimination of I. furcidens from portions of the Ayuquila River by invasive largemouth bass (Micropterus salmoides) and Poeciliopsis gracilis. Notes introduced population found in 2019 in Citala Reservoir. Species can reach 150 mm and is occasionally consumed as food in Mexico.

additional.name: Goodeid Working Group — Ilyodon furcidens
additional.notes: Specialist conservation hobbyist resource. Documented multiple wild population localities across Río Ameca, Río Ayuquila, Río de las Bolas, Arroyo de Ávalos, Río Jiquilpan, and other Mexican drainages. Noted taxonomic complexity (species includes parts formerly attributed to I. xantusi). Confirmed sexual dimorphism in wild populations.

fish.breedingNotes: The Trout Goodeid is viviparous, nourishing developing young internally via trophotaeniae — specialised placenta-like structures unique to the Goodeidae family. Females cannot store sperm and must be remated for each brood. Gestation lasts approximately 7 to 8 weeks, with broods typically numbering 5 to 50 large, well-developed fry that accept adult-sized foods from birth. Parental predation of fry tends to be low, though dense planting is recommended to improve fry survival in a community setting. Reliable reproduction generally depends on stable cool water in the 18 to 22°C range and a varied, plant-rich diet.
